data.title: Tesla Slips Further in California as Sales Decline for Sixth Straight Quarter
data.description:
Tesla stock fell over 3% on Wednesday following new data showing another drop in vehicle registrations in California, one of its most important markets. According to Experian Automotive, Tesla's car registrations in the state fell 15.1% year-over-year in Q1 marking the sixth consecutive quarterly decline.

This trend stands in contrast to the broader auto market in California, which saw total new vehicle registrations rise 8.3% during the same period. The California New Car Dealers Association (CNCDA) pointed to Tesla's direct-to-consumer sales model and CEO Elon Musk's public image as possible factors contributing to the brand's decline in appeal.

Tesla's share of the state's Zero Emission Vehicle market slipped to 43.9%, down 11.6% from the prior year. ZEVs now make up 20.8% of total car sales in the state, down from 22% a year ago signaling broader headwinds for the segment.

For the full year, overall new car registrations in California are projected to dip 2.3% to 1.71 million units. Toyota led the market with a 16.5% share, followed by Honda at 10.8%, with Tesla holding 9.1%. The data suggests shifting consumer preferences are complicating California's ambitious EV targets.

data.title: Foreign Central Banks Are Loading Up on T-Bills, Selling U.S. Bonds
data.description:
Karishma Vanjani

Foreign central banks, an essential group of buyers of U.S. government debt, continue to go all in on short-term Treasury bills while dumping longer-term U.S. debt at a swift pace.

For four straight months through February, central bankers abroad have sold Treasury bonds and notes, or debt that matures in over a year, the latest Treasury Department data show. It could be a sign that central bankers are more pessimistic on the long-term prospects of the U.S.

Foreign central banks unloaded a net $19.6 billion in longer-term U.S. bonds and notes in February, after selling $24.1 billion in January and $42.3 billion in December. A little over a billion was sold in November. The data were released as part of the Treasury International Capital report on Wednesday.

At the same time, foreign central banks bought a net $61.6 billion of Treasury bills, or shorter-term debt, in February. They bought $67 billion in January and $2.3 billion in December. The heavy appetite suggests that central bankers favor the yields on shorter-dated bills and are looking to shorten the duration of their portfolios.

"Another explanation would be to increase liquidity needs to fund potential currency intervention," wrote Angelo Manolatos, macro strategist at Wells Fargo, before the report was published.

This is breaking news. Read a preview of the Treasury International Capital report below and check back soon for more analysis.

Every month, the Treasury Department publishes past data on U.S. assets sold and bought by foreign central banks and investors. No ones typically sweats the numbers — but as President Donald Trump's trade policies continue to roil markets, investors must pay attention to the latest report.

The Treasury is set to release its Treasury International Capital data for February at 4 p.m. Eastern on Wednesday, an official confirmed with Barron's.

The report comes as fears about foreigners punishing Donald Trump's trade policies by selling U.S. assets has gripped financial markets. Stocks saw big swings last week, and the yield on the 10-year U.S. government bonds surged to 4.492% on Friday — its highest level since mid-February.

Higher yields mean someone, somewhere, is selling bonds.

The Treasury data can offer some clarity on foreign selling. Investors should pay special attention to the monthly data on transactions of long-term securities, which have a maturity that's more than one year old. This figure will show whether foreigners have been net sellers or buyers of U.S. Treasuries, corporate bonds, stocks, and other assets.

The report only shows market data from six weeks ago. However, given Trump started announcing global tariffs in February, the report could signal if foreigner central bankers and investors had a knee-jerk reaction to tariffs.

Treasury data so far show investors sold longer-term Treasuries for the three prior consecutive months. In January, foreigners sold a net $13.3 billion of U.S. notes and bonds, $49.69 billion in December, and $34.41 billion in November amid the U.S. election cycle.

Japan is the largest foreign holder of Treasuries; its holdings are down about $250 billion since late 2021. China, meanwhile, is the second-largest holder — and its holdings are at their lowest level since 2009.

Despite geopolitical pressures and past data, no one can say definitively that foreign nations are using their U.S. debt holdings as weapons of financial warfare. China's main state institutions own at least $3 trillion in dollar assets — and deliberately causing a selloff would be self-harming, wrote Mark Williams, Chief Asia Economist at Capital Economics on Monday.

Not only is it in China and other foreign countries' best interest to maintain the value of their U.S. holdings, they also have fewer options. The U.S. Treasury market is by far the safest and the largest in the world. Some of the U.S. companies selling stocks have market capitalizations that are more than a trillion dollars — surpassing the gross domestic product of many emerging nations.

"The only reason a fire sale could make financial sense would be if Chinese officials thought that the US government was about to freeze its assets or default on Chinese-owned bonds," Williams added.

It would be overkill to predict the U.S. will default on its debt, a catastrophic situation that's never occurred — and would almost certainly lead to a recession. However, the idea that the U.S. could freeze assets of foreign nations has heightened after Russia's U.S. funds were frozen in 2022, following the invasion of Ukraine.

Write to Karishma Vanjani at karishma.vanjani@dowjones.com.

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: FMC Receives Regulatory Approval in Peru for Keenali Herbicide
data.description:
FMC received regulatory approval in Peru for its Keenali herbicide with Dodhylex as the active ingredient.

Keenali is designed for use in Japonica and Indica rice, targeting resistant grass while ensuring crop safety, the company said Wednesday in a statement. The product is set to be available in Peru in August.

FMC submitted regulatory filings for Dodhylex in countries across Latin America and Asia, as well as in the US.

Shares of the company rose 1.9% in after-hours trading.

data.title: Grades mixed as crude stocks rise, domestic refinery demand grows
data.description:
Physically traded U.S. crude grades were mixed on Wednesday, dealers said, as buyers contended with growing refinery demand as well as a rise in crude inventories.

U.S. oil refiners are expected to have about 1.6 million barrels per day of capacity offline in the week ending April 18, increasing available refining capacity by 170,000 bpd, research company IIR Energy said.

Offline capacity is expected to fall to 1.2 million bpd in the week ending April 25, IIR added.

On the supply side, U.S. crude stocks rose last week despite a major increase in exports, while fuel inventories fell, the Energy Information Administration said.

Crude inventories rose by 515,000 barrels to 442.9 million barrels in the week ended April 11, the EIA said, compared with analysts' expectations in a Reuters poll for a 507,000-barrel rise.

* Light Louisiana Sweet (WTC-LLS) for May delivery rose 10 cents to a midpoint of a $2.25 premium and was seen bid and offered between a $2.00 and $2.50 a barrel premium to U.S. crude futures

* Mars Sour (WTC-MRS) fell 60 cents to a midpoint of a 90-cent premium and was seen bid and offered between an 80-cent and $1 a barrel premium to U.S. crude futures

* WTI Midland (WTC-WTM) fell 5 cents to a midpoint of a 85-cent premium and was seen bid and offered between a 75-cent and 95-cent a barrel premium to U.S. crude futures

* West Texas Sour (WTC-WTS) rose 3 cents to a midpoint of a 23-cent premium and was seen bid and offered between a 15-cent and 30-cent a barrel premium to U.S. crude futures

* WTI at East Houston (WTC-MEH), also known as MEH, traded between a $1.15 and $1.35 a barrel premium to U.S. crude futures

* ICE Brent June futures rose $1.18 to settle at $65.85 a barrel

* WTI May crude futures rose $1.14 to settle at $62.47 a barrel

* The Brent/WTI spread (WTCLc1-LCOc1) widened 9 cents to last trade at minus $4.01, after hitting a high of minus $3.91 and a low of minus $4.05

data.title: CSX 1Q Profit, Sales Fall Amid Operational ChallengesUpdate
data.description:
By Katherine Hamilton

CSX posted lower profit and revenue for the first quarter as the company said it faced operational challenges.

The railroad operator on Wednesday posted a profit of $646 million, or 34 cents a share, compared with $880 million, or 45 cents a share, a year earlier. Analysts polled by FactSet were expecting 37 cents a share.

Revenue fell 7% to $3.42 billion, missing analysts' expectations of $3.45 billion. The change was driven by declines in coal revenue, fuel surcharge and merchandise volume, CSX said.

Overall volume fell 1% to 1.52 million units. Coal volumes fell 9%, with revenue in that segment down 27%, on drops in production, including outages at customer facilities. Weather impacts on the overall supply chain also dragged that segment.

Trucking revenue decreased $13 million, or 6%, due to lower rates and fuel surcharge, CSX said.

"CSX faced operational challenges to start the year, which contributed to first quarter results that did not meet our expectations," Chief Executive Joe Hinrichs said.

Railroads are particularly vulnerable to shifts in international trade policy with about 38% of U.S. rail traffic tied to global trade, Stifel analyst Benjamin Nolan said in a note. Bad weather, particularly in February, also slowed networks, Benchmark analyst Nathan Martin said.

Write to Katherine Hamilton at katherine.hamilton@wsj.com

data.title: Asian Morning Briefing: U.S. Stocks Fall as Nasdaq Leads the Decline
data.description:
MARKET SNAPSHOT

U.S. stocks declined as tech stocks led a broader market slide and Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell said tariffs are "highly likely" to lead to a temporary rise in inflation. Treasury yields declined as Powell said tariffs could raise prices and unemployment at the same time, making decisions on interest rates harder to make. Oil traded higher, buoyed by signals that China could be open to negotiations over tariffs. The dollar fell and gold futures rose to a record high amid geopolitical and macroeconomic tensions.

MARKET WRAPS

EQUITIES

Stocks declined as traders reacted to a disclosure from Nvidia that it would take a $5.5 billion hit because future sales of its H20 AI accelerators to China would require a license from the U.S. Department of Commerce.

The Nasdaq Composite dropped 3.1% and the Dow Jones Industrial Average fell 1.7%. The S&P 500 fell 2.2%.

News that the Commerce Department will require a license to sell H20 chips to China is the latest sign that large tech companies, despite apparent efforts to appease the Trump administration, are still struggling to sway President Trump.

Nvidia said earlier this week it plans to build artificial intelligence supercomputers in the U.S. and spend up to $500 billion on AI infrastructure in the U.S.

Also, Powell said, "The level of the tariff increases announced so far is significantly larger than anticipated. The same is likely to be true of the economic effects, which will include higher inflation and slower growth."

Referring to the central bank's dual mandate of price stability and maximum employment, Powell warned, "we may find ourselves in the challenging scenario in which our dual-mandate goals are in tension."

Earlier Wednesday, Chinese shares ended mixed as semiconductor companies led the gains among the major stocks.

The benchmark Shanghai Composite Index gained 0.3%, the Shenzhen Composite Index fell 1.1% and the ChiNext Price Index declined 1.2%. Hong Kong's Hang Seng Index declined 1.9%.

Japanese stocks ended lower as concerns about U.S. tariffs and their economic implications persisted. Chip and electronics stocks led the losses. The Nikkei Stock Average fell 1%

Australia's S&P/ASX 200 held steady.

New Zealand's NZX-50 rose 0.5%.

COMMODITIES

Oil futures increased, supported by a weaker dollar and a Bloomberg report that Beijing would be open to negotiate under certain conditions regarding trade tensions.

According to the report, citing a person familiar with the Chinese government, Beijing would be open to talks if the Trump administration showed more respect and adopted a more consistent position.

"A return to some kind of 'normal' trade relationship between the two largest economies in the world would be a positive price development that could postpone some of the more dire forecasts for oil prices," Mizuho's Robert Yawger said in a note.

However, further gains seem to be capped by persistent concerns over the impact of U.S. tariffs on global economic growth and energy demand, with market participants in wait-and-see mode.

West Texas Intermediate settled up 1.9% at $62.47 a barrel. Brent crude gained 1.8% to $65.85 per barrel.

April gold gained 3.4% to $3,326.60 per troy ounce.

It was the largest one-day percentage gain since April 9, 2020. Gold was up in six of the past seven sessions.

Gold's latest rally to another record comes as the U.S. looks to further isolate China from global trade, SP Angel analysts said in a note. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent has pitched the idea of extracting concessions from U.S. trading partners to isolate China, and reports of removing Chinese stocks from U.S. exchanges have not been ruled out. China is holding off negotiations with the U.S. despite Trump suggesting a deal is possible.

The U.S. dollar has resumed its downward trend and ETF demand is picking up, further strengthening gold's appeal, SP Angel added.

TODAY'S TOP HEADLINES

Powell Warns of 'Challenging Scenario' for Fed as Trade War Rages

Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell warned that the central bank could face difficult trade-offs in trying to cushion the U.S. economy from the fallout of President Trump's trade war.

Large tariff increases that push up consumer prices while weakening economic activity would put the central bank in the uncomfortable position of choosing whether to focus on its goal of promoting low inflation or its mandate to foster a healthy labor market, Powell said in remarks prepared for delivery in Chicago on Wednesday.

"We may find ourselves in the challenging scenario in which our dual-mandate goals are in tension," Powell said.

WTO Sees Fall in Trade Flows, Warns Against Spreading Conflict

Global trade in goods will fall this year, and the decline could be severe if President Trump presses ahead with suspended tariff hikes and uncertainty about trade policy spreads beyond the U.S., the World Trade Organization said Wednesday.

The Geneva-based dispute resolution body expects exports and imports of goods to fall by 0.2% this year as a result of the tariff rises implemented by April 14, both in the U.S. and in retaliating countries. It had previously forecast growth of 2.7%. The WTO's economists see a continued if less severe hit in 2026, lowering their growth projection to 2.5% from 2.9%.

In its twice-yearly report on the trade outlook, the WTO said cross-border sales of goods could fall by 0.8% if tariff rises on countries around the world that were announced by the U.S. on April 2 but then suspended for 90 days are implemented. Additionally, should businesses become as uncertain about the trade policies of other major economies as they are about those of the U.S., the decline could deepen to 1.5%.

March Retail Sales Beat. Consumers Are Stocking Up Ahead of Tariffs.

Spending to get ahead of tariffs made March a sunnier month for retail sales, which came in higher than projected. But uncertainty about the import taxes continues to cloud the outlook.

Retail sales gained 1.4% in March from February, better than economists' forecasts for a 1.3% increase, the Census Bureau said Wednesday.

March sales rose 4.6% from a year ago.

Cleveland Fed's Hammack Says the Market Selloff Is Different This Time

Cleveland Federal Reserve President Beth Hammack said Wednesday that policymakers must consider the possibility that economic, and even market, conditions may not follow normal patterns going forward. Echoing Dorothy Gale's infamous line from The Wizard of Oz, Hammack said "we may not be in Kansas anymore."

In prepared remarks to the Columbus Metropolitan Club Weekly Forum on Wednesday, Hammack said President Donald Trump's tariff hikes constitute a "substantive change" in trade policy and it will take time to work through the uncertainty caused by these changes.

"Financial markets have been volatile as they incorporated this news into various asset prices," Hammack said of recent market reactions. Before joining the Cleveland Fed in August, Hammack was at Goldman Sachs and has worked in the financial markets for 30 years.

Nvidia Is Now the Biggest U.S.-China Bargaining Chip

Nvidia's position in artificial-intelligence computing is strong enough that even the company's bottom-shelf chips have plenty of demand. In the trade war, this isn't proving to be a good thing.

The company's share price fell hard Wednesday after Nvidia told investors the U.S. government now requires a license for it to sell its H20 AI chips in China and a handful of other countries. The company said this will lead it to take a current-quarter $5.5 billion charge related to inventory and purchase commitments for the chip.

The inventory charge indicates about $13 billion in actual H20 sales, according to Tim Arcuri of UBS. Nvidia generated $115.2 billion in data-center revenue in its fiscal year that ended in January-and is projected to do about $182 billion in sales for that segment this year, according to FactSet estimates.

data.title: Powell does not think Trump's labor board firings case will apply to Fed
data.description:
Key points:
  • Case over Trump firings being closely watched in Fed circles
  • Powell: 'never going to be influenced by any political pressure'
  • Powell's remarks on independence draw audience applause

The legal case before the Supreme Court over President Donald Trump's firing of two Democrats from federal labor boards does not likely apply to the Federal Reserve, the head of the U.S. central bank said on Wednesday.

Fed Chair Jerome Powell, in response to a question at an event at the Economic Club of Chicago, said "that's a case that people are talking about a lot. I don't think that that decision will apply to the Fed, but I don't know. It's a situation that we're monitoring carefully."

Powell said the Fed's independence is "very widely understood and supported in Washington and in Congress where it really matters."

The matter is raised regularly to Powell during public appearances because his relationship with Trump is fractious. Elevated from member of the Board of Governors to central bank head by Trump during his first term in the White House, Powell soon fell out of favor with the Republican president, who repeatedly threatened to try to fire him for not bowing to his demands at the time to cut interest rates.

"The point is we can make our decisions and we will only make our decisions based on our best thinking ... our best analysis of the data about what is the way to achieve our dual mandate goals as we can to best serve the American people," Powell said. "That's the only thing we're ever going to do. We're never going to be influenced by any political pressure."

"People can say whatever they want. That's fine. That's not a problem, but we will do what we do strictly without consideration of political or any other extraneous factors," he concluded, a line that drew several seconds of applause from the audience.

Powell's remarks come a day after lawyers told the Supreme Court in filings connected to the case over Trump's firings of two Democratic appointees to government labor boards that allowing the dismissals to stand leaves the independence of the Fed vulnerable. The case is being closely followed by economists and other Fed-watchers for its potential implications for the U.S. central bank.

It "would greenlight chaos, including the potential destruction of the independent Federal Reserve on which the national economy relies," lawyers for Cathy Harris, whom Trump fired from the Merit Systems Protection Board, wrote in a brief to the court urging the justices to reject the administration's case. Lawyers for Gwynne Wilcox, dismissed from the National Labor Relations Board, made similar arguments.

The Supreme Court last week cleared the way for Trump to remove Harris and Wilcox for now. The stay of an appellate court ruling that they should be reinstated gives the nine justices time to consider the Trump administration's formal request to block lower courts' orders to reinstate them while litigation over the firings continues.

The Fed system's seven governors, including the system chair, are appointed by the president and confirmed by the Senate. The Federal Reserve Act of 1913 that created the modern U.S. central bank stipulates they may be dismissed only "for cause," not for political or policy disagreements.

data.title: Earnings Flash (SNV) Synovus Financial Q1 Sales $570.9 Million vs. FactSet Est Sales $569.8 Million
Tech, Media & Telecom Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Technology, Media and Telecom. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1449 ET - Companies in the semiconductor industry beyond Nvidia will be affected by Trump's trade restrictions for the graphic chip maker's H20 chips, BofA analyst Vivek Arya tells WSJ. "Nvidia has multiple partners when it brings a product out, so if there are restrictions to sell that product, that entire supply chain gets impacted," Arya says. Some of the companies that are being hit include KLA and ASML, who help provide equipment to make these chips. "There is that ecosystem that gets impacted," Arya says. Overall, the global industry has a problem--China is the largest buyer of semiconductors and the U.S. is its largest designer, Arya says. Nvidia shares drop 8.8% to $102.38. KLA and ASML fall 4.4% and 6.7%, respectively. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

1409 ET - New restrictions on selling semiconductors to China could significantly reduce the market opportunity for American chip companies, DA Davidson analyst Gil Luria tells WSJ. "While the extent of the restrictions will only be clear once the U.S. publishes the details, Nvidia's disclosure indicates the restrictions are much more severe than they were before," Luria says. Nvidia slides 9%, and is the worst performer in the S&P 500. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

1353 ET - If the goal of U.S. government restrictions on Nvidia's sales of H20 chips in China was to restrict Beijing's AI capabilities, that ship has already sailed, BofA Securities analyst Vivek Arya tells WSJ. "I understand the goal is to restrict AI, but limiting this product is no way limiting China's AI capability because Huawei Technologies can make products that have similar capabilities," Arya says. The risk is that if Huawei takes its products and is able to sell them into other emerging markets and other places outside of China at a very low price, there is nothing to prevent them from doing that. And it wouldn't help the trade gap either, he adds. "It's hard to defend these moves." Nvidia slides 8%. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

1301 ET - Interactive Brokers' clients have deleveraged slightly this month, Chief Executive Milan Galik said during the company's earnings call, helping fuel a downward move for the stock. But Citi analysts say positive account growth remains the key metric for the company's long-term story. In a research note, they point to the company's continued expansion of commission revenues and its ability to drive year-over-year growth in net interest income despite lower rates. Galik said the company saw a significant increase in the number of approved accounts on its platform so far this month. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1149 ET--The timeline shared by Nvidia Tuesday afternoon regarding Trump's imposed China-related restrictions for its H20 chips explains the rushed announcement that the graphic chip maker would build $500 billion of AI infrastructure over the next four years, DA Davidson analysts say in a research note. The appeal to the Trump administration was aimed at mitigating the final regulation, the analysts say. "Nvidia may now choose not to follow through on this pledge given the outcome, and if it does, we believe it will erode long-term margins," the analysts add. Nvidia shares fall 6.4% to $105.03. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

1129 ET--Nvidia's $5.5 billion charge related to Trump's export restrictions to its H20 chips won't only have a significant impact on the company's first-quarter gross margins, but also its revenue growth during the entire fiscal year, William Blair analysts say in a research note. "While we still believe that Nvidia is the leader in AI infrastructure, with a significant technology moat and robust demand for its next-generation BlackWell GPUs, this will only add to the wall of worry that has built up around the name over the last few months," the analysts say. Shares may be range-bound in the near term as macro uncertainty persists, the analysts say. However, they urge investors "not to miss the forest for the trees" when it comes to a secular winner of the AI platform shift. Shares fall 6.8% to $104.61. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

1041 ET - Nvidia may be using the U.S. government's new H20 license requirement as a concession in talks with the Trump administration to get the AI Diffusion Rule taken off the table, UBS analysts say in a research note. The rule, which aims to control the export and deployment of advanced chips and related technologies, is set to go into effect am May 15, according to the analysts. They say they struggle to see why Nvidia would follow through on a commitment to build $500 billion in AI infrastructure in the U.S. over the next four years if it faces both the H20 restriction, which they say is effectively a ban, and isn't able to kill the AI Diffusion Rule. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1000 ET - For the 20th consecutive month, rents declined in March, and the median asking price for rent in the 50 largest metros is now $65 lower than the 2022 peak, standing at $1,694, according to Realtor.com. While rents have been declining for nearly two years due in large part to new multi-family inventory, new tariffs could have impacts on metros where multi-family permitting activity is growing the fastest, jeopardizing rent declines. "In nearly every major U.S. metro, rents are still considerably higher than 2019," says Realtor.com. The recently announced tariffs on imported building materials such as steel and aluminum could potentially impact the multi-family housing supply by driving up construction costs. Markets that experienced rapid growth in permitted multi-family homes are expected to see the biggest impacts as developers and builders may postpone or even cancel new projects. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

0828 ET - Wedbush says the Trump administration's move to restrict next generation AI chips to China is a "strategic blow" for Nvidia and that the company "now has massive blockades going after the China market in the middle of this raging US/China tariff battle." The analysts say in a note that "the Street will take this news with clear nervousness worried these are the first shots fired in the tech battle between the US and China and Beijing/Xi are not just going to take this news and walk away." Nvidia slides 6.4% to $104.94 in premarket trading. Chip stocks in general are all lower this morning with Broadcom, Qualcomm, Micron, Marvell, AMD all weaker. ASML Holding is also lower after 2Q sales guidance came up short.(denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

0546 ET - Ericsson should be a good relative performer in 2025 given its exposure to more stable telecom operator spending and given the risks to other hardware stocks, JPMorgan analyst Sandeep Deshpande writes. That said, margin risks have increased as the high-margin U.S. market represented 45% of revenue in the first quarter and it's not clear how much customers increased their buying ahead of tariffs. The pre-buy effect could reverse in the second half, he says. Second-quarter margin guidance is better than expectations, aided by a partial Lenovo contract settlement. Given the radio access network market is unlikely to grow in the next few years, investor focus will be on returns, not growth. "Ericsson could make the stock really interesting with a buyback." Shares fall 0.9%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0501 ET - Investors prefer U.K. stocks over U.S. stocks, Bank of America's global fund managers survey for April shows. Investors also favor bonds, utilities and staples, while they are underweight the tech sector, the energy sector and industrials, according to the survey. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0449 ET - Bitcoin edges lower after Nvidia said it faces a $5.5 billion hit from tighter U.S. rules on shipments of its new artificial intelligence chip to China. The microchip maker will require licences to export its chip to China as the U.S.-China trade war escalates. "Trump also launched a probe into whether critical minerals should face tariffs, so that added to fears that further tariffs were on the horizon," Deutsche Bank analysts say in a note. That raises fears that the trade war could still get worse from here, they say. Bitcoin falls 0.9% to $83,215, according to LSEG. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

data.title: Health Care Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Health Care sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1332 ET - The European Commission approved Eisai and Biogen's drug Leqembi to treat certain patients with early stage Alzheimer's disease in the European Union. However, TD Cowen analysts say in a research note that it's unlikely the new approval will have a material effect on Biogen's books. "We suspect that the EU launch is likely to be slow and to be impeded by many of the logistical challenges that have impacted the U.S. launch," they write. As a result, they are not making changes to estimates.(connor.hart@wsj.com)

1108 ET - Abbott Laboratories management considered raising earnings guidance for the year given the momentum they were seeing in the company's base business, but opted to reaffirm the outlook in light of uncertainty around President Trump's tariffs, CEO Robert Ford says on a call with analysts. The company estimates that the tariffs could have an impact of "a few hundred million dollars," Ford says, with most of that coming in the back half of the year. He expects there might be some mitigating factors offsetting that impact, including a weakening U.S. dollar or other foreign exchange shifts. "I can tell you there's not a lot of R&D slowing down or SG&A slowing down in those mitigation plans," the CEO says. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

0640 ET - Fewer investors find European bank stocks more attractive than they did before April, Bank of America's European fund manager survey for April shows. Investors prefer defensive stocks such as utilities and healthcare. The April survey shows 30% of investors find European banks attractive, down from 50% in March. A 26% share of investors expect European banks to face pressure, up from 13% in March. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0442 ET - Sartorius's 1Q beat was driven by strong consumables sales, whereas equipment sales remained soft, Intron Health analysts say in a note. While the analysts ponder whether tariff-driven customer stocking played a role, all major regions excluding China contributed at least high single-digit growth. Shares in the German life-science company rise 5.9% to 205.60 euros. (helena.smolak@wsj.com)

0435 ET - The unquantified beat in Sartorius's bioprocess solutions orders, a critical metric for investors, is a major positive, Morgan Stanley analysts say in a note. The German life science company's 2025 outlook provides limited insight into the potential effects of tariffs on margins but suggests a limited financial impact. Its 1Q results are a continuation of trends from 2H 2024, in line with recent management commentary during its annual results, the analysts say. Shares rise 2.6% to 199.25 euros. (helena.smolak@wsj.com)

0412 ET - Sartorius's better-than-expected 1Q results and a quantified 2025 outlook reassure investors, reflecting a solid market recovery, Jefferies analysts say in a note. The German life-science company's issued 2025 guidance of 6% sales growth and underlying Ebitda margin of around 29%-30%, which should be well-received by investors, they say. Shares are up 3.2% at 200.50 euros. (helena.smolak@wsj.com)

0257 ET - Praram 9 Hospital likely has high earnings growth with potential upside, Maybank Securities (Thailand)'s Nontapat Sahakitpinyo says in a research report. The Thailand hospital operator should deliver decent earnings in 1Q, supported by strong international revenue, the analyst says. Its 2Q momentum should remain robust on a yearly and quarterly basis, underpinned by Middle East patient inflows post-Ramadan, the analyst says. A tax benefit of THB15 million is also expected in 2Q or 3Q from medical equipment donations to public hospitals, the analyst adds. The brokerage has a buy rating and a target price of THB32.00 on the shares, which are 3.6% higher at THB23.30. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0255 ET - Ipsen's first-quarter sales came in ahead of consensus expectations after a better-than-expected result for its Somatuline cancer drug, RBC Capital Markets analysts say in a research note. The drug's sales were robust, benefiting from continuing shortage of generics competitors, and beat expectations by 14%, according to RBC. "Clearly, it is likely that supply improves through the year and thus sales could still move into a decline in [the second half], but this is a good start," the analysts say. The outlook for Somatuline and potential impacts from U.S. tariffs are likely to be in focus at the company's earnings call, RBC says. Ipsen reiterated its full-year guidance. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

2139 ET - UOB KayHian cuts its year-end KLCI target to 1620 from 1690, citing persistent U.S.-China tariff tensions, high U.S. bond yields, and potential "Trump tantrums" that weigh on valuations and recession risks. Although Trump rolled back extreme tariff threats, escalating trade frictions may still dampen sentiment, analysts say in a note. They remain defensive but see near-term trading opportunities. Tech remains vulnerable, but relief on phones, PCs, and chips opens tactical entry points in oversold names like Inari Amertron and NationGate, they say. Malaysia's lower tariff exposure also supports trade diversion, likely benefiting electronic manufacturing services players, UOB KH adds. Top picks include Eco World Development, Gamuda, Hartalega, and Hong Leong Bank. The KLCI is flat at 1486.60. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

data.title: Financial Services Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Financial Services. Exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1301 ET - Interactive Brokers' clients have deleveraged slightly this month, Chief Executive Milan Galik said during the company's earnings call, helping fuel a downward move for the stock. But Citi analysts say positive account growth remains the key metric for the company's long-term story. In a research note, they point to the company's continued expansion of commission revenues and its ability to drive year-over-year growth in net interest income despite lower rates. Galik said the company saw a significant increase in the number of approved accounts on its platform so far this month. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1209 ET - Interactive Brokers has boosted its crypto offerings after the Securities and Exchange Commission loosened its stance on the industry, Chief Executive Milan Gilak said during the company's earnings call. But with its expanded offerings, Interactive Brokers isn't seeing as much interest in the space as expected, he said. "It's not growing as fast as we would like, which to me personally is somewhat of a surprise," he said, adding the cost of trading crypto assets on its platform is lower than what its competitors charge. The company added four new cryptocurrencies last quarter and three more this week, bringing its total offering to 11 cryptocurrencies. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1101 ET - Travelers says the direct impact from tariffs enacted by the Trump administration should be pretty manageable. CEO Alan Schnitzer on an analyst call says the impact is "just a fraction of auto and property losses that are physical damage related and only a fraction of those are for materials that would be impacted by tariffs." He adds that the most significant affect for the company is likely to be one-time impacts of physical damage repair costs, which most notably affects private passenger auto. "From there, the impacts diminish pretty significantly." Shares rise 3.9% to $259.47 following a 1Q earnings beat despite losses from California wildfires. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1038 ET - European insurers form a relatively defensive subsector and would be resilient to a recession, RBC Capital Markets says in a research note. "Our constructive view is supported by 'staple like' demand, continued earnings power and healthy balance sheets," its analysts write, noting that insurers have tightened their risk management and pricing discipline in recent years. European asset managers are, conversely, among the worst-performing names. This is due to the impact on their assets under management, which is tied to their earnings, and the sentiment turning more risk-off, RBC adds. Investor reticence to gain exposure in this space for now is understandable, the analysts say.(elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0640 ET - Fewer investors find European bank stocks more attractive than they did before April, Bank of America's European fund manager survey for April shows. Investors prefer defensive stocks such as utilities and healthcare. The April survey shows 30% of investors find European banks attractive, down from 50% in March. A 26% share of investors expect European banks to face pressure, up from 13% in March. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0501 ET - Investors prefer U.K. stocks over U.S. stocks, Bank of America's global fund managers survey for April shows. Investors also favor bonds, utilities and staples, while they are underweight the tech sector, the energy sector and industrials, according to the survey. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0446 ET - Wise could face revenue hurdles under the possibility of higher U.S. tariffs, Peel hunt analysts Gautam Pillai and Barun Singh write in a note. This might lead to a widespread growth and consumer spending slowdown, the analysts say. "The company remains inherently consumer-focused, with a significant share of personal cross-border flows tied to discretionary spending categories such as travel, holidays, and overseas gifting." Any pullback in these categories can weigh on transaction volumes and hurt the money-transfer company's top-line growth, they say. Shares are down 1.6% at 955.50 pence. (najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

0351 ET - Italy's opinion on whether to use its so-called golden powers to allow UniCredit's takeover of smaller peer Banco BPM could come earlier than expected and with conditions, Equita says in a research note after two Italian newspapers reported the decision could land in the next few days. A conditional approval is reportedly likely, with limits on branch reductions and on the rationalization of investment banking activities, guarantees on its headquarters' location and a potential exit from Russia. "These elements do not, in our opinion, diminish the industrial sense of the deal; however, it will be necessary to understand their magnitude in detail in order to properly assess the impact on the generation of synergies and value creation from the transaction," analyst Andrea Lisi writes. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0304 ET - EQT shares could see an initial positive reaction to strong comments around BPEA fundraising, especially given the high short interest, JPMorgan analysts write. However, the outlook on exits, fund valuations and fundraising could potentially drive consensus EPS downgrades. "Hence we remain cautious." The company had a strong first quarter, but the outlook points to slowdown. EQT's fee-generating assets under management rose to 142 billion euros, 3% above consensus on stronger fundraising of 12.4 billion euros. This was driven by the activation of BPEA IX fund earlier than expected, JPMorgan says. EQT now expects a "material slowdown" in exit activity, its listed holdings have declined by 9% quarter-to-date, and industry-wide fundraising volumes are not expected to return to 2021 levels until 2027. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0241 ET - Nordea reported another solid result with pre-provision profits coming in 4% ahead of consensus, Jefferies analyst Alexander Demetriou writes. A positive was the 2% beat on net interest income, with volumes coming in better than expected, he says. Fees were below while costs were better. Pretax profit came in 7% ahead and the CET1 ratio of 15.7% was 20 basis points ahead, he says. Nordea continues to target a return on equity of above 15% in 2025 with consensus currently at 14.5%. Jefferies rates Nordea at buy with a 13 euro price target. Shares closed at 11.15 euros. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

2139 ET - UOB KayHian cuts its year-end KLCI target to 1620 from 1690, citing persistent U.S.-China tariff tensions, high U.S. bond yields, and potential "Trump tantrums" that weigh on valuations and recession risks. Although Trump rolled back extreme tariff threats, escalating trade frictions may still dampen sentiment, analysts say in a note. They remain defensive but see near-term trading opportunities. Tech remains vulnerable, but relief on phones, PCs, and chips opens tactical entry points in oversold names like Inari Amertron and NationGate, they say. Malaysia's lower tariff exposure also supports trade diversion, likely benefiting electronic manufacturing services players, UOB KH adds. Top picks include Eco World Development, Gamuda, Hartalega, and Hong Leong Bank. The KLCI is flat at 1486.60. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

data.title: Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1501 ET [Dow Jones]--Oil futures defy a tech-led slide in equities and post gains, held up by a report that China is willing to talk trade with the U.S. under certain conditions, further losses in the U.S. dollar, and a smaller-than-expected build in U.S. crude-oil stockpiles. "A return to some kind of 'normal' trade relationship between the two largest economies in the world would be a positive price development that could postpone some of the more dire forecasts for oil prices," Mizuho's Robert Yawger says in a note. The Treasury Department, meanwhile, stepped up enforcement against Iran with sanctions against a second Chinese independent refinery for buying what it said was more than $1 billion of Iranian oil. WTI settles up 1.9% at $62.47 a barrel and Brent rises 1.8% to $65.85 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0959 ET - With Parkland's CEO stepping down one day after shareholder Simpson Oil again called for an overhaul of the fuel distributor and retailer, TD Cowen says Simpson's hand is now even stronger going into the May 6 shareholder meeting. Simpson is battling to elect an alternate slate of directors. Analyst Michael Van Aelst says that Bob Espey's tenure was one of Simpson's sticking points and, coupled with another earnings miss, could clear the way for Simpson to gain control of the board. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

0919 ET - Crude futures extend gains, supported by dollar weakness and the market encouraged by a Bloomberg report that China would negotiate with the U.S. on tariffs under certain conditions. Macroeconomic headwinds will likely limit price recovery despite some optimism around potential tariff rollbacks, while rising U.S. crude inventories suggest ample supply, George Pavel of Naga.com Middle East says in a note. "Without stronger support from broader market conditions, crude oil prices could remain near their lows," he says. The API reported a 2.4 million barrel crude stock build for last week, analysts note. Closely watched EIA inventory numbers are due at 10:30 a.m. ET. WTI and Brent are up 1.1% at $62.01 and $65.38 a barrel, respectively. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0640 ET - Fewer investors find European bank stocks more attractive than they did before April, Bank of America's European fund manager survey for April shows. Investors prefer defensive stocks such as utilities and healthcare. The April survey shows 30% of investors find European banks attractive, down from 50% in March. A 26% share of investors expect European banks to face pressure, up from 13% in March. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0501 ET - Investors prefer U.K. stocks over U.S. stocks, Bank of America's global fund managers survey for April shows. Investors also favor bonds, utilities and staples, while they are underweight the tech sector, the energy sector and industrials, according to the survey. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0341 ET - Allocation to bonds shows a record increase, according to Bank of America's April global fund manager survey. Relative to history, investors are overweight bonds, but also to utilities, staples and U.K. stocks, BofA says in an accompanying press statement. The rush to bonds is caused by U.S. capital flight and global fiscal panic. Alongside the record increase in allocation to bonds, global equity allocation fell to its lowest since July 2023. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

2009 ET - Oil prices edge higher in the early Asian session on possible position adjustments. However, dimming prospects of trade deals amid the global tariff conflict may weigh. Negotiations between the EU and the U.S. have made little progress toward a deal, ANZ Research analysts say in a research report. Also, "Trump administration officials have indicated that the 20% reciprocal tariff, as well as other tariffs targeting sectors such as cars and metals, will not be removed outright," the analysts add. Front-month WTI crude oil futures are up 0.2% at $61.47/bbl; front-month Brent crude oil futures are 0.2% higher at $64.82/bbl. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

data.title: CIVISTA BANCSHARES, INC. DECLARES SECOND QUARTER COMMON DIVIDEND
data.description:
SANDUSKY, Ohio, April 16, 2025 /PRNewswire/ — Civista Bancshares, Inc. ("Civista") announced today that the Board of Directors has approved a quarterly dividend of 17 cents per common share, equivalent from the prior quarter, to shareholders of record date April 29, 2025, payable May 13, 2025.

This dividend represents a payout of approximately $2.7 million. Based on the Civista's closing stock price of common shares of $19.54 on March 31, 2025, the quarterly dividend produces an annualized yield of 3.38%.

About Civista Bancshares, Inc.:

Civista Bancshares, Inc., is a $4.1 billion financial holding company headquartered in Sandusky, Ohio. Its primary subsidiary, Civista Bank, was founded in 1884 and provides full-service banking, commercial lending, mortgage, and wealth management services. Today, Civista Bank operates 42 locations across Ohio, Southeastern Indiana and Northern Kentucky. Civista Leasing & Finance, a division of Civista Bank, offers commercial equipment leasing services for businesses nationwide. Civista Bancshares' common shares are traded on the NASDAQ Capital Market under the symbol "CIVB". Learn more at www.civb.com.

This press release may contain forward-looking statements regarding the financial performance, business prospects, growth and operating strategies of Civista. For these statements, Civista claims the protections of the safe harbor for forward-looking statements contained in the Private Securities Litigation Reform Act of 1995. Statements in this press release should be considered in conjunction with the other information available about Civista, including the information in the filings we make with the Securities and Exchange Commission.

data.title: Basic Materials Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Basic Materials. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0525 ET - Rio Tinto's 1Q copper production was 6% above consensus expectations due to higher grades at its Escondida mine, while bauxite production was 5% above due to a strong performance in Guinea, Barclays analysts write. Overall, the miner delivered a soft quarter as Australian shipments of iron ore were weak due to losses sustained from four cyclones, they write. Its London-listed shares trade down 1.2% at 4,299 pence. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0424 ET - 'Long gold' ends a 24-month streak for 'long Magnificent 7' as the most crowded trade, Bank of America's April global fund manager survey shows. 'Long gold' is cited as the most crowded trade by 49% of investors surveyed by BofA, followed by 24% mentioning 'long Magnificent 7', a reference to Apple, Microsoft, Alphabet, Amazon, Nvidia, Meta and Tesla stocks. Gold is also expected to be the best performing asset in 2025 according to 42% of investors, up from 23% in March. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0315 ET - Antofagasta's first-quarter copper production was 2% lower than expected due to a weaker performance at its Los Pelambres mine, which was impacted by a national power outage and maintenance, RBC Capital Markets analysts Ben Davis and Marina Calero write. Its average copper price $4.69 a pound was 13% higher than expected, they write. Antofagasta's permit for its Zaldivar mine is due to expire in May and its renewal is going to the wire, however, there is no indications that it shouldn't be received in time, they add. Shares fall 2.2% at 1,502 pence.(adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

2237 ET - Rio Tinto bull Jefferies says it is a buyer of the miner's stock at current prices, despite a clearly challenging 1Q operationally and risks from Trump's trade war. It says Rio's Australia-listed stock trades at an EV/Ebitda of 5.0X based on Jefferies's 2026 estimates and a free cash flow yield of 6.4%. "We would expect Rio shares to be defensive as market volatility likely persists in the near term," Jefferies analysts say in a note. "These shares also offer leverage to an eventual cyclical recovery, although that probably doesn't happen until 2026." Rio Tinto is down 1.7% in Sydney at A$109.51 after reporting a fall in 1Q iron-ore shipments. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

2103 ET - Rio Tinto's 1Q Pilbara iron-ore shipments were impacted by cyclones more than Citi expected, says analyst Paul McTaggart. Shipments of 70.7 million metric tons are down 17% on the quarter immediately prior, while the lower-grade SP10 product represents "a high" 32% of shipments, he says. Iron ore contributes most of Rio Tinto's profits. Production of other commodities is broadly in line, "but noting Escondida refined copper production was impacted by the Chile-wide power outage," McTaggart says. He adds that "lithium was a one-liner for Rio in this report," following its recent acquisition of Arcadium Lithium. Citi has a neutral rating and A$130.00 target on Rio Tinto, which is down 1.3% at A$109.94. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

data.title: Auto & Transport Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Auto and Transport sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1339 ET - The strength in March U.S. retail sales isn't only about consumers trying to get ahead of tariffs, Wells Fargo's Tim Quinlan and Shannon Grein write. Sales beat WSJ consensus in both the headline and the ex-auto figures. The report included upward revisions of previous data. Autos saw the biggest March gain, likely reflecting a rush to buy vehicles before any price increase due to tariffs. But consumers "also spent more at restaurants in March, and we're not aware of a way you can front-run tariffs with a nice night out," Quinlan and Grein say. "How consumers act in these next couple of months will be telling," they say. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1014 ET - United Airlines' earnings indicated stability in forward bookings, giving analysts hope the company can withstand fears of an economic downturn. Several airlines said in March they were seeing falling consumer demand due to economic uncertainty, but United said Tuesday advanced ticket sales rose by almost $3 billion from the prior quarter, a good sign for forward bookings, TD Cowen analysts Tom Fitzgerald and Helane Becker say. Premium and international booking are driving that with 17% and 5% growth respectively. While holiday travel could be helping the bookings figures, the numbers are still solid as investors look for a potential deterioration in international booking from tariffs, BofA analyst Andrew Didora says. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

0854 ET - Treasury yields are little changed from yesterday's settle after March retail sales beat expectations. Sales increased 1.4%, while economists surveyed by WSJ expected 1.2%. February's figure was unrevised at 0.2%. Sales ex-autos rose 0.5%, versus consensus of 0.3%. The data may reflect consumers anticipating purchases to get ahead of tariffs. Industrial production data is due at 9:15 a.m. ET. Fed Chair Powell has a speech at 1:30 p.m. and investors will likely watch for hints on how tariffs might be affecting interest rate decisions. The 10-year yield is at 4.326% and the two-year at 3.812%. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

0530 ET - BMW could be one of the first names that investors consider to play any bounce in European autos, Citi analysts write. The bank has been cautious for the past few years, firstly as BMW was over-earning during Covid, and latterly due to BMW's high China exposure. Whilst BMW's 5%-7% auto EBIT margin guidance is encouraging, the 17% first quarter decline in China sales highlights continued pressure. Nevertheless, BMW had a positive pre-earnings call, shares are down 13% since tariffs began, and it holds around 70 euros per share in net cash. "Whilst we prefer Renault and Volkswagen on fundamental EPS momentum, we think BMW could lift from current levels as tariff overreaction recedes." Shares fall 1.2%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0513 ET - XPeng is expected to further develop its autonomous driving technology, Nomura analysts write in a note. The entire auto market in China is likely to enter the "autonomous driving 3.0 era" from the 2.0 era, they add. XPeng also targets mass production of its flying cars and humanoid robots within 2026, they say. The technology and features updates will likely support XPeng in maintaining its market momentum into the rest of this year, Nomura says. The company showed an active business plan, with the upcoming G7 and Mona M03 Max in addition to its G01 model, they say. Nomura maintains a positive stance on XPeng's outlook for the foreseeable future. The company could see some sequential improvement for its monthly sales and further share gains in China, they add. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0507 ET - Malaysia's auto sector could face subdued growth prospects amid intensifying competition and currency volatility, CIMB Securities analyst Mohd Shanaz Noor Azam says in a note. A prolonged U.S.-China trade conflict could weaken the ringgit and lift input costs, straining margins and dampening big-ticket spending like car purchases, he reckons. Chinese EV makers are expected to ramp up their presence in Malaysia, raising pressure on local and non-Chinese foreign brands, he adds. However, national brands like Perodua are seen as more resilient due to affordability and their high proportion of local content, Shanaz says. CIMB maintains a neutral rating on Malaysia's auto sector, pegging Sime Darby as its top pick. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0424 ET - 'Long gold' ends a 24-month streak for 'long Magnificent 7' as the most crowded trade, Bank of America's April global fund manager survey shows. 'Long gold' is cited as the most crowded trade by 49% of investors surveyed by BofA, followed by 24% mentioning 'long Magnificent 7', a reference to Apple, Microsoft, Alphabet, Amazon, Nvidia, Meta and Tesla stocks. Gold is also expected to be the best performing asset in 2025 according to 42% of investors, up from 23% in March. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0240 ET - Maersk shares are attractively valued despite consensus assuming unprecedented negativity in the ocean unit, Citi analysts write. The stock is cheap on many valuation metrics, and while it's not cheap using the P/E measure, this is because the outlook for ocean is challenging, it adds. In Citi's view, the market may be overlooking Maersk swinging from net debt of $12.5 billion at the end of 2019 to net cash of $6.4 billion at the end of 2024. The market is concerned about trade demand and excess capacity, but Citi notes the potential for higher levels of vessel scrapping and idling and greater responsiveness to challenging conditions. Citi upgrades Maersk to neutral from sell and raises its target price to 11,093 Danish kroner from 9,500 kroner. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0240 ET - China's domestic market for autos will continue to outshine this year, thanks to an effective government stimulus, Daiwa analysts write in a note. The Shanghai Auto Show in late April is a near-term catalyst, they say. Daiwa revises up its China EV outlook this year, driven by stronger-than-expected battery EV demand. Daiwa now expects China's EV market to grow 33% on year. The current U.S. tariffs on auto imports from China remain applicable, they say. Some China's auto component names may benefit from the potential pause of a 25% tariff on the auto industry in the near term, Daiwa says. The brokerage expects limited impact from tariffs on Chinese automakers as most have already given up their U.S. market since China-U.S. trade war started. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

2129 ET - HL Mando's 1Q results were likely dampened by limited output increases by its local auto maker clients, Daiwa Capital analysts Yoonki Bae and Henny Jung write in a note. The South Korean car-component supplier may also have suffered from production line adjustments at U.S. electric-vehicle makers, the analysts say. Mando's 1Q revenue and operating profit likely fell 10% and 27%, respectively, on quarter, they note. Still, they remain optimistic about Mando's 2025 growth. The impact of U.S. tariffs on Mando will be minimal as its American clients are responsible for import clearance, they add. Daiwa cuts the stock's target price by 9% to KRW50,000 but keeps a buy rating. Shares are 2.7% lower at KRW36,500. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

1724 ET - United Airlines said air-travel demand is weaker but stable — and that weakness is mainly coming from domestic travel. The airline said domestic passenger revenue per available seat mile (PRASM) fell 4% in the first quarter and domestic passenger load slipped 3.4 percentage points. Meanwhile, international PRASM climbed 5% and international passenger load rose almost two percentage points. Forward bookings over the past two weeks have stayed stable, United said, with international up 5%. CEO Scott Kirby said in March that a 50% drop in government-related travel had rippled into domestic leisure travel, which was also on a decline. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1717 ET - United Airlines is relying on its loyalty program to stay resilient through a potential economic downturn, it said in its earnings report. The airline said revenue from its premium-cabin seating rose 9.2% in the first quarter despite what it called a challenging macroeconomic environment. That was compared with 7% to 8% growth in business and basic-economy revenue. Focusing on brand loyalty has been United's strategy since Covid, it said, and it intends to continue accelerating investments there. "United believes our proven ability to win brand-loyal customers is a competitive advantage and will make United resilient in any economic environment," it said. Shares rise 5.9% in late trading. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

data.title: Stocks Plummet on Semiconductor Weakness and Powell Comments
data.description:
The S&P 500 Index Wednesday closed down -2.24%, the Dow Jones Industrials Index closed down -1.73%, and the Nasdaq 100 Index closed down -3.04%. June E-mini S&P futures (ESM25) are down -2.12%, and June E-mini Nasdaq futures (NQM25) are down -2.83%. 

Stock indexes on Wednesday sold off sharply. Semiconductor stocks plunged on Wednesday to lead the overall market lower. Nvidia closed down more than -6% after the US government barred the sale of its H20 chips to China, with Nvidia saying the ban will cost it $5.5 billion in Q1 tied to inventory and commitments for the chips. Also, ASML Holding NV closed down more than -7% after reporting that Q1 bookings were below consensus. Stocks added to their losses Wednesday after the WTO cut its 2025 global trade estimates.

Join 200K+ Subscribers: Find out why the midday Barchart Brief newsletter is a must-read for thousands daily.

The rout in stocks deepened Wednesday afternoon as the indexes sold off further on remarks from Fed Chair Powell, who warned that trade tensions could spur inflation and slow growth, and that the Fed was in no hurry to cut interest rates.

Wednesday's US economic news was mostly better than expected and was a supportive factor for stocks. Stocks also found some support from a Bloomberg report that said China wants to see a number of steps from the Trump administration before it will agree to trade talks, including showing more respect by reining in disparaging remarks by members of his cabinet.

US MBA mortgage applications fell -8.5% in the week ended April 11, with the purchase mortgage sub-index down -4.8% and the refinancing mortgage sub-index down -12.4%. The average 30-year fixed rate mortgage rose +20 bp to 6.81% from 6.61% in the prior week.

US March retail sales rose +1.4% m/m, right on expectations and March retail sales ex-autos rose +0.5% m/m, stronger than expectations of +0.4% m/m.

US March manufacturing production rose +0.3% m/m, slightly stronger than expectations of +0.2% m/m.

The US Apr NAHB housing market index unexpectedly rose +1 to 40, stronger than expectations of a decline to 38.

The World Trade Organization (WTO) cut its 2025 global trade forecast to a -0.2% decline from a November forecast of up +3.0% and warned if the US pushes ahead with reciprocal tariffs, global trade will contract -1.5% this year. 

Cleveland Fed President Hammack said a "modestly restrictive" policy stance is appropriate, and there is a "strong case" for the Fed to hold interest rates steady to balance risks. 

Fed Chair Powell said the Trump administration's tariffs are even higher than the Fed's upside scenario, and the US economy will likely be "moving away" from both of the Fed's goals of full employment and stable prices for "probably for the balance of this year." He added, "For the time being, we are well-positioned to wait for greater clarity before considering any adjustments to our policy stance."

Last Friday, President Trump said he would temporarily exempt consumer electronics from reciprocal tariffs and the baseline 10% global tariffs. However, a 20% tariff still applies to electronics shipped from China. Last Wednesday, President Trump announced a 90-day pause on higher reciprocal tariffs on 56 nations but left the new 10% baseline tariff on virtually all nations in place. Meanwhile, the EU last Thursday said it will delay for 90 days the implementation of 25% tariffs on 21 billion euros worth of US goods sent to Europe.

Stocks have been under pressure over the past five weeks due to fears that US tariffs will weaken economic growth and corporate earnings. On March 4, President Trump imposed 25% tariffs on Canadian and Mexican goods and doubled the tariff on Chinese goods to 20% from 10%. On April 2, President Trump signed a proclamation to implement a 25% tariff on US auto imports. The tariffs will initially target vehicles fully assembled outside the US and, by May 3, will expand to include automobile parts made outside the US. On April 5, a 10% baseline tariff for virtually all nations took effect. Last Friday, China raised tariffs on all US goods to 125% from 84% in retaliation for the US raising tariffs on Chinese goods to 145%. 

The US tariff turmoil has undercut the dollar and boosted gold. The dollar index last Friday sank to a 3-year low, and gold prices soared to an all-time high Wednesday. The markets are concerned about the effects of US trade policies, which have caused consumer confidence to plummet and have prompted many companies to suspend their capital spending plans, a negative factor for GDP growth. Also, the dollar is facing a confidence crisis as the US weaponizes tariffs, diminishing the dollar's reserve-currency status and prompting some foreign investors to liquidate their dollar assets. 

The markets will focus on US trade policy news during this holiday-shortened week. On Thursday, Mar housing starts are expected to fall -5.7% m/m to 1.416 million, and Mar building permits are expected -0.6% m/m to 1.450 million.

The markets are discounting the chances at 19% for a -25 bp rate cut after the May 6-7 FOMC meeting.

Q1 earnings reporting season began last Friday as big US banks reported their results. According to data compiled by Bloomberg Intelligence, the market consensus is for Q1 year-over-year earnings growth of +6.7% for the S&P 500 stocks, down from expectations of +11.1% in early November. Full-year 2025 corporate profits for the S&P 500 are seen rising +9.4%, down from the forecast of +12.5% in early January. 

Overseas stock markets on Wednesday settled mixed. The Euro Stoxx 50 closed down -0.08%. China's Shanghai Composite rose to a 1-1/2 week high and closed up +0.26%. Japan's Nikkei Stock 225 closed down -1.01%.

Interest Rates

June 10-year T-notes (ZNM25) Wednesday closed up +11 ticks. The 10-year T-note yield fell -7.0 bp to 4.263%. June T-notes Wednesday recovered from early losses and settled moderately higher. Short covering emerged in T-notes Wednesday after stocks plunged, sparking safe-haven buying of government debt. T-notes added to their gains Wednesday afternoon on strong demand for the Treasury's $13 billion auction of 20-year T-bonds with a bid-to-cover ratio of 2.63, better than the 10-auction average of 2.59. T-notes also had carryover support from rallies in European government bonds after UK March consumer prices rose less than expected.

T-note prices on Wednesday initially moved lower based on stronger-than-expected US economic news on retail sales, manufacturing production, and the NAHB housing market index. Also, supply pressures weighed on T-notes as the Treasury auctioned $13 billion of 20-year T-bonds Wednesday. 

European bond yields Wednesday moved lower. The 10-year German bund yield fell to a 1-week low of 2.479% and finished down -2.6 bp to 2.509%. The 10-year UK gilt yield slid to a 1-week low of 4.571% and finished down -4.5 bp to 4.603%.

UK Mar CPI rose 2.6% y/y, weaker than expectations of +2.7% y/y. Mar core CPI rose +3.4% y/y, right on expectations.

Swaps are discounting the chances at 97% for a -25 bp rate cut by the ECB at Thursday's policy meeting.

US Stock Movers

Chip stocks plunged on Wednesday and weighed on the broader market. Advanced Micro Devices closed down more than -7% to lead losers in the Nasdaq 100. Also, ASML Holding NV closed down more than -7% after reporting weaker-than-expected Q1 bookings. Nvidia closed down more than -6% to lead losers in the Dow Jones Industrials after the US government banned it from selling its H20 chip to China. In addition, GlobalFoundries and KLA Corp closed down more than -5%, and Applied Materials , Lam Research , and ON Semiconductor Corp closed down more than -4%. Finally, Intel , Broadcom , and Texas Instruments closed down more than -3%, and Marvell Technology , ARM Holdings Plc , and NXP Semiconductors NV closed down more than -2%.

The Magnificent Seven stocks retreated Wednesday and pressured the broader market. Tesla closed down more than -4%, and Apple , Meta Platforms , and Microsoft closed down more than -3%. Also, Amazon.com and Alphabet closed down more than -2%.

Wednesday's +1% rally in WTI crude oil lifted energy producers. APA Corp closed up more than +3% to lead gainers in the S&P 500, and Devon Energy closed up more than +2%. Also, Diamondback Energy closed up more than +2% to lead gainers in the Nasdaq 100. In addition, Occidental Petroleum , Marathon Petroleum , and Conoco Phillips closed up more than +2%, and Exxon Mobil and Schlumberger closed up more than +1%.

Interactive Brokers Group closed down more than -9% after reporting Q1 total net interest income of $770 million, below the consensus of $794.3 million. 

JB Hunt Transport Services closed down more than -7% to lead losers in the S&P 500 after reporting Q1 Final Mile Services revenue of $200.7 million, weaker than the consensus of $220 million. 

Omnicom Group  closed down more than -7% after reporting a Q1 operating profit of $452.6 million, weaker than the consensus of $485.1 million. 

AGCO Corp closed down more than -5% after Morgan Stanley downgraded the stock to underweight from equal weight with a price target of $75. 

Abbott Laboratories closed up more than +2% after reporting Q1 adjusted EPS of $1.09, stronger than the consensus of $1.07 and confirming its full-year guidance.

Travelers Cos closed up more than +1% to lead gainers in the Dow Jones industrials after reporting Q1 core EPS of $1.91, well above the consensus of 74 cents.

Autoliv closed up more than +4% after reporting Q1 adjusted EPS of $2.15, well above the consensus of $1.65. 

Newmont closed up more than +2% after BMO Capital Markets reinstated coverage on the stock with a recommendation of outperform with a price target of $63.

Lockheed Martin closed up more than +1% after Morgan Stanley upgraded the stock to overweight from equal weight with a price target of $575.

Earnings Reports (4/17/2025)

data.title: Soybean barge basis eases on sluggish spot demand
data.description:
Basis bids for soybeans delivered by barge to U.S. Gulf Coast terminals eased on Wednesday, amid lukewarm spot demand and as soybean buyers looked to the South American crop, traders said.

* CIF corn basis bids also eased, offsetting support from a weakening U.S. dollar, traders said.

* Traders are watching for updated news on U.S. President Donald Trump's trade war with China, as the dispute has accelerated China's shift toward soybeans from Brazil, where farmers are finishing a massive harvest.

* U.S. corn farmers are set to plant one of their biggest areas in history this year.

* But weekend rains will stall plantings in the southern and eastern Midwest, Commodity Weather Group said. Larger delays are expected in the Midwest and Delta regions toward the end of April, the firm said.

* The USDA is due to release weekly export sales data on Thursday morning.

* CIF Gulf soybean barges loaded in April were bid down 3 cents at 79 cents over CBOT May futures; May loadings were bid down 3 cents at 78 cents over futures.

* Export premiums for soybeans loaded in April were steady at about 94 cents over May futures, while May shipment offers also were steady at about 87 cents over futures.

* CIF Gulf corn barges loaded in April were bid at 73 cents over CBOT May corn (CK25) futures, down 2 cents from Tuesday.

* FOB export premiums for corn shipped in April were steady, offered at about 87 cents over May corn futures, while May shipment offers also were steady at about 86 cents over futures.

data.title: AMD, Micron, Broadcom Stocks Drop as U.S. Tightens Chip Export Rules
data.description:
By Elsa Ohlen

Chip stocks were falling after the U.S. imposed new licensing requirements on some artificial-intelligence semiconductor exports to China. The iShares Semiconductor exchange-traded fund closed down 3.9%.

The U.S. government will require licenses for the export of Nvidia's H20 and Advanced Micro Devices' MI308 AI chips, as well as their equivalents, for the indefinite future.

AMD stock dropped 7.4% to $88.29 on Wednesday. The chip maker said in a filing that it would book up to $800 million in charges as a result of the export controls. Nvidia, whose shares finished 6.9% lower, said it would take a $5.5 billion charge in the current quarter because of the restrictions.

"A warning from AI chips champion Nvidia that it will face a $5.5 billion hit from tightened U.S. controls on exports to China marks a new chapter in the escalating tit-for-tat between Washington and Beijing," AJ Bell investment director Russ Mould said.

The headlines weighed on shares of other chip makers, too. Intel, Qualcomm, Micron Technology, Broadcom, and Marvell Technology all fell between 2% to 4%.

Write to Elsa Ohlen at elsa.ohlen@barrons.com

data.title: CME cattle, hog futures rise on fund buying
data.description:
Chicago Mercantile Exchange cattle and hog futures moved higher on Wednesday as a weaker dollar and a seasonal uptick in demand supported prices, traders said.

Retailer demand for beef and pork has increased as the start of the grilling season approaches.

CME June live cattle futures (LCM25) settled up 2.275 cents to end at 202.075 cents per pound and May feeder cattle futures (FCK25) ended 1.875 cents higher to 284.4 cents per pound.

In the beef market, choice cuts of boxed beef fell $1.37 to $334.06 per hundredweight (cwt) and select cuts fell 45 cents to $314.79 per cwt on Wednesday morning, according to data from the U.S. Department of Agriculture.

The dollar index moved back toward three-year lows reached after U.S. President Donald Trump announced his tariff policies.

A weakening dollar makes U.S. agricultural products more competitive globally.

"The dollar getting cheaper makes commodities more attractive," Matthew Wiegand, broker at FuturesOne, said. "Money is rotating back to commodities."

Expectations that the USDA's Thursday cattle on feed report will show tight cattle stocks have also helped fuel a rebound in prices, an analyst said.

U.S. April 1 cattle on feed are estimated to be down 1.8% from a year ago, a Reuters poll of analysts showed.

CME June lean hog futures (LHM25) settled up 2.775 cents to 97.95 cents per pound. Hog futures have also increased on steadying equities markets, analysts said.

The USDA priced pork carcasses rose 89 cents to $92.62 per cwt.
